critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The year-over-year MAPE comparison for the load forecasting model has three confounds that prevent attributing the error reduction to methodological improvement.

The interruptible-load contracts signed between the two measurement years allow demand response during peak hours. Flatter, controllable peaks are genuinely easier to forecast — the uncertainty associated with extreme peak-load hours is reduced when those loads can be curtailed. A simpler target distribution produces lower MAPE regardless of model sophistication.

The TOU tariff revision shifted commercial cooling behavior between the two years. This is a structural change to the demand side that alters the hourly load pattern. Year-over-year MAPE comparison is interpretable only if the underlying load process is stationary. The TOU change violates this assumption.

The weather vendor switch — providing better station coverage — improves the exogenous inputs to the model. A better weather forecast input produces better load forecast outputs. This is an input quality advantage, not a methodology advantage.

The conclusion that the new approach 'generalizes better to modern grid conditions' and should replace the legacy forecaster across all balancing areas cannot be supported by the year-over-year comparison. The methodology claim requires controlled evaluation: both models on the same historical intervals with the same weather data.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
